stories.headline: Update: Downtown Denver assault victim dies; suspect faces first-degree murder charge
stories.summary:
A man died from injuries sustained in a violent unprovoked attack Saturday evening in the 1300 block of Larimer Street near Speer Boulevard in downtown Denver. A witness told 911 dispatchers they observed suspect Edgardo Jimenez-Alvarad, 30, repeatedly stomp on an unresponsive man before lifting a large rock and throwing it onto the victim's head. Officers arrived around 6:30 p.m. and transported the victim to a hospital, where he was later pronounced dead.

Jimenez-Alvarad was taken into custody minutes after the attack near 14th and Champa streets based on a detailed description provided by a witness. Investigators recovered a large rock believed to have been used in the assault. During a formal interview, the witness told detectives there was no verbal or physical altercation before the attack and that the victim never moved.

Jimenez-Alvarad was booked into the Denver County Jail on suspicion of first-degree murder. Police have not released the identity of the victim. The case represents a significant escalation from the previously reported assault, as the victim has now died and formal murder charges have been filed.

stories.headline: Update: Green Ridge Fire near Steamboat Springs grows; Fishhook Fire also breaks out as Polis expands disaster declaration
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis has issued a disaster declaration for Routt County in response to the Green Ridge Fire, which ignited Sunday afternoon near Stagecoach Reservoir about 15 miles south of Steamboat Springs. The fire spread rapidly to 94.3 acres by Sunday evening, prompting mandatory evacuations for multiple zones and the opening of an evacuation center at Soroco High School in Oak Creek. By Monday, the fire had not grown overnight and containment reached 27%, allowing some evacuation orders to be downgraded to pre-evacuation status.

A second fire, the Fishhook Fire, broke out Sunday around 12:30 p.m. off Rabbit Ears Pass on U.S. Forest Service land and grew to an estimated 144 acres by late Monday. Steamboat Ski Resort activated 41 snowmaking guns to wet terrain around Tomahawk and Spike trails and near Rendezvous Lodge, with the Fishhook Fire burning approximately three miles from the resort boundary.

The disaster declaration activates the State Emergency Operations Plan and directs the Department of Public Safety, including the Division of Homeland Security and Emergency Management and Division of Fire Prevention and Control, to oversee response and recovery efforts. The Green Ridge Fire is threatening nearly 480 structures, according to the wildfire tracking platform WatchDuty. Residents in pre-evacuation zones were advised to pack vehicles and keep gas tanks full.

stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres fire approaches 100,000 acres at 36% containment as heat and wind increase threat
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire burning in Pueblo and Custer counties grew to an estimated 98,609 acres and reached 36% containment as of Monday morning, with fire officials warning that hot, dry conditions forecast for the week will test containment lines. Crews are using heavy equipment to build fire lines on the west side of Rye to protect the community from active fire near North Muddy Creek, and firefighters are also working to contain smoldering pockets in the northeast threatening to ignite juniper trees. The fire is burning less than two miles from the town of Rye.

Evacuated residents of Beulah and Wetmore were permitted to begin returning to their properties on a scheduled basis starting Monday. Fire Chief Ross Gallegos of the Rye Fire Protection District warned in a letter to the community that fire activity has remained unpredictable, with repeated periods of increased intensity, and that it is not yet fully safe to return. Officials expect the fire to continue burning for months, possibly until winter.

The Gold Mountain fire near Ouray also continued to grow, reaching 36,259 acres at 11% containment as of Monday morning. A heavy-lift helicopter conducting water drops from Silver Jack Reservoir crashed Sunday evening, killing the pilot. Crews are reinforcing fire lines along the Highway 550 corridor and building bulldozer lines along the west side of Cimarron Ridge to protect threatened structures.

stories.headline: Proposed Front Range passenger rail rebranded as 'Colorado Connector' ahead of 2029 launch
stories.summary:
The Front Range Passenger Rail District unveiled a new name, logo and brand identity Sunday for the proposed Front Range rail line, which will be called Colorado Connector, or CoCo. The first phase of service, expected to launch in 2029, will operate three daily lines between Denver and Fort Collins. Full service connecting 12 communities from Pueblo to Fort Collins using existing freight rail lines is planned for 2032, contingent on voter approval of a sales tax increase.

Initial service will be operated by Amtrak, which will provide expertise in crews, ticketing, maintenance and federal safety compliance. A trip from Fort Collins to Denver is estimated at approximately one hour and 30 minutes, with a Denver-to-Pueblo trip taking about two hours and 30 minutes. Funding will come from a combination of federal grants, state funding, local revenue and the potential voter-approved sales tax.

The projected cost of the project ranges between $3 billion and $3.5 billion, according to the Front Range Passenger Rail District website, though General Manager Sal Pace has declined to provide an exact figure, citing the need to finalize financial and phasing plans. Unanswered questions about total cost and long-term funding remain a point of scrutiny for the project.

stories.headline: Denver City Council advances bill to extend nightlife venue hours to 4 a.m.
stories.summary:
Denver City Council advanced a bill on a 10-1 vote Monday that would allow certain nightlife venues to remain open until 4 a.m., two hours beyond the current closing time. Alcohol service would still be required to end at 2 a.m. under state law, but music, dancing and other entertainment could continue for the additional two hours. Proponents argue the change would reduce congestion caused by bar crowds hitting the streets simultaneously at 2 a.m.

The measure, put forward by the city's Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection, would overhaul Denver's entertainment venue licensing structure. It would replace existing licenses for amusement, dance halls, social rooms and cabarets with three new license categories — limited entertainment business, nightlife entertainment business and adult entertainment business — effective April 1, 2027. Two of the new categories would require enhanced security measures including video surveillance, weapons pat-downs and a designated manager registered with the city.

The lone dissenting vote came from Councilmember Amanda Sawyer, who raised concerns about the impact on residential neighborhoods in districts such as Cherry Creek, Lowry and portions of Colfax, where entertainment venues sit directly adjacent to homes. Representatives of the Lower Downtown Neighborhood Association also urged the council to consider a trial period before full implementation. The bill will advance to a second reading before the full council.

stories.headline: Aldi files permits for first two Denver stores, part of plan to open roughly 50 Colorado locations
stories.summary:
German discount grocer Aldi has filed building permits for two locations in the Denver metro area, marking the chain's first foothold in Colorado. One store is planned at 9111 E. 40th Ave. near Interstate 70 and Central Park Boulevard, spanning approximately 19,400 square feet with 109 parking spaces. A second location is proposed at 18453 E. 57th Ave. near the Rocky Mountain Arsenal National Wildlife Refuge and the 61st/Peña Rail Station, covering nearly 20,000 square feet with 90 parking spaces.

Aldi announced earlier this year that it plans to open approximately 50 stores across the Denver and Colorado Springs area within two years. Colorado was one of 11 states without an Aldi location prior to that announcement. Both permit applications, filed July 1, include beer and wine sales. The chain did not respond to requests for comment on opening timelines.

Aldi is among the fastest-growing grocery chains in the United States and was cited during the Kroger-Albertsons merger trial as a potential major competitor in the Colorado market. The discount grocer is known for private-label products, low prices and requiring shoppers to use a quarter-deposit cart system. Its American subsidiary, Aldi Süd, has operated in the U.S. since 1976.

stories.headline: Colorado health insurance marketplace to lose Cigna, gain Colorado Access in 2027
stories.summary:
Colorado's individual health insurance marketplace will see a significant reshuffling in 2027 as Cigna Healthcare exits and Colorado Access, a local nonprofit insurer, joins the exchange. Cigna announced it would pull out of all states where it offered individual plans, as that segment represented a small share of its overall business. Colorado Access will offer plans in 14 counties, including most of the 11 where Cigna currently sells coverage, with the exception of Larimer and Weld counties.

Colorado Access currently administers Child Health Plan Plus in 45 counties and Medicaid in Adams, Arapahoe, Denver and Douglas counties. CEO Annie Lee said the state's efforts to stabilize the marketplace — including a legislative decision to contribute $80 per month per enrollee for those earning less than four times the poverty level — made it viable for the nonprofit to enter. The last time a new insurer joined the marketplace was 2024, when Utah-based Select Health began offering plans.

The marketplace has faced headwinds this year after pandemic-era subsidies expired at the end of December, driving a roughly 7% drop in enrollment through February compared to the prior year. Colorado's decline was smaller than the national average of 12%. Lee also noted that expected Medicaid coverage losses tied to new federal eligibility verification requirements under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act could push former Medicaid enrollees into the individual marketplace, representing a potential new customer base for Colorado Access.

stories.headline: Rockies close first half 20 games below .500 after 3-1 loss to Giants; McCarthy's inside-the-park homer a bright spot
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies ended the first half of the 2026 season on a low note, falling 3-1 to the San Francisco Giants at Oracle Park on Sunday to complete a 2-5 California road trip. Jake McCarthy hit a leadoff inside-the-park home run in the first inning, but the Rockies managed just four hits and two walks the rest of the game. The loss dropped Colorado to 39-59, matching its season-worst mark of 20 games below .500.

McCarthy's homer was the second inside-the-park leadoff home run of his season, making him the first player in nearly a century to accomplish that feat in a single year. The last player to do so was Edd Roush of the New York Giants in 1929. Giants starter Trevor McDonald went seven innings, allowing just three hits.

Antonio Senzatela, who had a 1.13 ERA over his first 15 appearances this season, yielded the go-ahead runs in the eighth inning after a throw from catcher Hunter Goodman sailed into center field. Senzatela's ERA over his last 15 appearances has ballooned to 7.41. Starter Michael Lorenzen pitched five innings of one-run ball but continued to struggle with walks, issuing three free passes to raise his total to 12 over his last three starts.

stories.headline: Rockies analyze full MLB Draft haul: pitching arsenals, versatility and two catchers headline 21 picks
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies completed their 21-player MLB Draft over the weekend, with new general manager Paul DePodesta and president Josh Byrnes using the picks to signal their organizational priorities: pitching depth, defensive versatility and athleticism behind the plate. The club took switch-hitting shortstop Tyler Bell of Kentucky with the 10th overall pick for $6.39 million, following last year's fourth-overall selection of Ethan Holliday at the same position. Both players will continue developing as shortstops, though officials said they will play multiple positions.

The Rockies also selected two catchers in the first three rounds — Golden Spikes Award winner Daniel Jackson of Georgia at No. 37 and Jack Natili of Cincinnati at No. 76 — along with pitchers Logan Reddemann at No. 38 and Ben Davis, a catcher-turned-pitcher from Mississippi State, at No. 104. Jackson batted .379 with 32 home runs, 87 RBI and 26 stolen bases in 67 games last season, winning the SEC Triple Crown and leading the nation in total bases and runs scored.

DePodesta said the draft board fell favorably for Colorado, giving the club opportunities at players it valued. Assistant GM Tommy Tanous emphasized versatility as a core organizational building block, noting that the team needs players capable of moving around the diamond as the rebuild continues. The upcoming August 3 trade deadline will offer additional insight into the front office's direction.

stories.headline: Avalanche sign forward Fabian Lysell to one-year deal for 2026-27 season
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche announced Monday that they have signed forward Fabian Lysell to a one-year contract for the 2026-27 season. Lysell, 23, was acquired by Colorado in a trade with the Boston Bruins on June 27. He spent last season with the AHL's Providence Bruins, recording 42 points — 17 goals and 25 assists — in 57 regular-season games.

Lysell, a native of Gothenburg, Sweden, has accumulated 163 career AHL points in 219 regular-season games, all with Providence. He has appeared in 12 NHL games for the Bruins, recording three points. The 5-foot-11, 185-pound forward was originally drafted by Boston in the first round, 21st overall, in the 2021 NHL Draft.

The signing gives the Avalanche a young offensive option with AHL pedigree as the team works within a tight salary cap situation, currently projected at roughly $104.4 million in cap hits. Colorado's cap picture has drawn attention amid speculation about whether the team can accommodate a potential Cale Makar extension as the salary cap continues to rise. Lysell represented Sweden at two IIHF World Junior Championships.

stories.headline: Colorado Parks and Wildlife officers kill uncollared wolf in northern Colorado
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife officers were forced to take lethal action against an uncollared wolf in northern Colorado, the agency confirmed. Details on the specific location, date and circumstances surrounding the action were not fully disclosed in initial reports. The incident adds a new development to the state's ongoing wolf reintroduction program, which has faced both logistical and community relations challenges since reintroduction began.

The killing of an uncollared wolf raises questions about how wildlife managers track and monitor individual animals within the growing Colorado wolf population. Because the animal was uncollared, officials lacked the telemetry data typically used to monitor wolf movements and behavior. CPW has not yet released a full statement explaining what prompted the lethal action.

The wolf reintroduction program has been a focal point of tension between ranching communities and wildlife advocates across the state. Range riders and other protective measures have been deployed to help reduce livestock conflicts. This incident is likely to draw renewed scrutiny from both supporters and opponents of the program.

stories.headline: Museum of Contemporary Art Denver to open 28-artist 'Colorado Currents' exhibition July 24
stories.summary:
The Museum of Contemporary Art Denver will open a major new exhibition, "Colorado Currents," on July 24, featuring work by 28 artists connected to Colorado as the state marks its 150th anniversary. More than 100 pieces will be on display through November 1, with 44 of them newly commissioned works. Co-curators Miranda Lash and Leilani Lynch spent 18 months traveling the state and consulting with artists and arts organizations before selecting participants.

The exhibition is organized around three loose themes — nature, history and personal identity — displayed across the museum's three floors. Works include oil paintings documenting wildfire burn scars by Erika Osborne, projections referencing the Marshall fire by Erin Hyunhee Kang, and miniature plein-air landscape paintings by Evan Forrest Mann. Curators said they deliberately sought artists asking regionally specific questions beyond the traditional landscape imagery that dominates Colorado-themed art.

The show grew from a longlist of approximately 300 Colorado-connected artists, later narrowed in consultation with RedLine Contemporary Arts Center, Tilt West and the Fine Arts Center in Colorado Springs. Lash said the institution's core mission is to introduce new ideas, and the curatorial team worked to ensure the exhibition covered a broad range of subjects and perspectives tied to the state's history and current challenges.

weather.notable: Nearly 6 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: The instructions on the back of shampoo bottles are getting way too complicated. 'Lather, rinse, repeat.' Repeat? How long am I supposed to be in here?

topStory.headline: Trump Imposes 20% Toll on Strait of Hormuz Transit as U.S.-Iran Strikes Escalate, Sending Oil Prices to One-Month High
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump announced the United States would assume control of the Strait of Hormuz and impose a 20% toll on eligible cargo passing through the critical waterway, declaring the U.S. would henceforth be known as 'The Guardian of the Hormuz Strait.' The announcement came amid a third consecutive day of U.S. strikes on Iran and Iranian retaliatory attacks on commercial vessels, U.S. allies, and regional infrastructure. Brent crude rose sharply on the news, climbing above $86 a barrel to a one-month high, still below the nearly $120 peak reached earlier in the conflict but well above the $72.48 level recorded before U.S.-Israeli strikes on Tehran began in late February.

U.S. Central Command confirmed it had carried out strikes targeting Iranian forces it said were responsible for attacking commercial shipping in the strait, with Trump directing the operations to hold Iranian forces accountable. Iran retaliated with missile attacks on Bahrain, which sounded its alert siren for a third consecutive day, as well as attacks on Jordan and oil tankers associated with the United Arab Emirates. Iranian cruise missiles struck the MT Al Bahiyah and MT Mombasa tankers near Oman's coast, killing at least one mariner and wounding others, prompting India, which lost a national in the attack and had three others killed in a separate U.S. strike in June, to summon an Iranian diplomat and issue a formal protest.

Separately, strikes hit the port city of Bushehr, home to Iran's only civilian nuclear power plant, with Iranian authorities blaming the United States, though CENTCOM said it had completed its latest round of strikes before the Bushehr attack occurred. Additional unclaimed airstrikes on Iran raised questions about whether other actors are also targeting the Islamic Republic. In Yemen, Iran-backed Houthi rebels launched missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Abha International Airport after blaming Riyadh for airstrikes on Sanaa International Airport, marking the first major Saudi-Houthi escalation following a prolonged period of relative calm.

The number of vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz has fallen sharply, with data analytics firm Kpler recording only six crossings on Sunday, the fewest in five weeks. The strait normally carries approximately one-fifth of the world's oil supply. Global financial markets reacted negatively, with South Korea's Kospi falling 8%, Japan's Nikkei 225 and China's Shanghai Composite each dropping roughly 2%, and chip stocks hit particularly hard, with SK Hynix shares falling 15% and Samsung Electronics down 10%.

The United Nations Security Council held an emergency session Monday, with U.N. Assistant Secretary-General for Political Affairs Khaled Khiari warning that 'Yemen and the wider region cannot afford another cycle of escalation' and calling on all parties to engage in negotiations under U.N. auspices. Goldman Sachs analysts cautioned that recent attacks underscore how uncertain Gulf exports remain and that serious re-escalation could sharply intensify upside risks to oil prices in the near term. A fragile interim U.S.-Iranian truce signed last month appears increasingly strained as both sides continue exchanging strikes.
